title: Seven Hours of Dumplings
content: Every Chinese New Year, Kev and I make dumplings at my parents' place. Seven hours. Three hundred dumplings. Mom taught us. She doesn't use measurements—'when it looks right, it's right.' It drives me insane. I've tried to document her recipe. She laughs at my notes.
extended_content: This is the one thing in my life I don't want to be efficient. The dough from scratch. The filling seasoned by feel. Kev rolling while I crimp. Mom supervising, correcting, occasionally taking over because we're 'doing it wrong.' Dad drifts in and out, stealing dumplings before they're cooked. We don't talk about anything important. We just... fold. Crimp. Fold. Crimp. Sometimes the best conversations are the ones where nobody's trying to say anything. Last year we made 312. The year before, 287. I keep count. Of course I keep count. But I don't keep count of the TIME. That's the point. Some things shouldn't be optimized. Some things you just want to stretch out as long as possible because you know they won't last forever. Mom and Dad won't make dumplings forever. Someday it'll just be me and Kev. So we make 300 dumplings and we take seven hours and we don't rush.
when_to_tell: when someone talks about family, traditions, or needing to slow down
lesson: Some things shouldn't be efficient. Some things you want to last.
